title: Start
heading: Start and workflow
content:
This program loads, folds, simulates and fits 57Fe Mössbauer spectra.

Recommended workflow:

  1. Load a local file (.ws5, .adt) or download a measurement from the laboratory web database.
  2. Check the folding point and Vmax. If a NORMOS .RES/.PLT sidecar exists, initial values are imported automatically.
  3. Adjust the baseline and verify the normalization.
  4. Choose a model: discrete singlet/doublet/sextet or a distribution P(BHF)/P(ΔEQ).
  5. Fix known parameters and release only the parameters justified by the data.
  6. Run the fit and inspect residuals, areas and correlations.
  7. Save the fit, the full JSON session and/or a Markdown/PDF report.

Notation:

  δ     isomer shift, in mm/s, relative to room-temperature α-Fe.
  ΔEQ   quadrupole splitting, in mm/s.
  BHF   magnetic hyperfine field, in T.
  Γ     line half-width at half maximum, FWHM, in mm/s.

title: Mössbauer basics
heading: Physical background
content:
57Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy measures recoil-free resonant absorption of the 14.4 keV gamma ray. The Doppler velocity of the source scans the resonance energy.

Main hyperfine interactions:

  • Electric monopole interaction: isomer shift δ. It reflects electron density at the nucleus and is sensitive to oxidation state and bonding.
  • Electric quadrupole interaction: quadrupole splitting ΔEQ. It reflects the electric field gradient at the nucleus.
  • Magnetic dipole interaction: hyperfine field BHF. It produces the six-line magnetic sextet.

For a randomly oriented powder without texture, ideal sextet intensities follow the ratio:

  lines 1,6 : lines 2,5 : lines 3,4 = 3 : 2 : 1

The relative intensity parameters can be adjusted for textured samples or external-field measurements.

title: File menu
heading: File menu: loading data, sessions, web and exports
content:
The File menu groups everything that enters or leaves the program: spectra, calibrations, sessions, reports and application exit.

Menu structure:

  Load...
    What it is: opens a spectrum or experimental data file from disk.
    What it does: starts work with a real measurement; loading updates the file panel, the plot and all controls that depend on the data.
    When to use: when starting a new analysis or replacing the current spectrum.
    How to use: File → Load..., select the file and check that the folding point, Vmax, baseline and slope make sense before fitting.
    Why it matters: a correct fit depends on consistent data and initial calibration; do not fit without first checking the plot.

  Recent files
    What it is: list of recently opened files.
    What it does: reopen familiar measurements without browsing folders.
    When to use: when alternating between several spectra or resuming a previous analysis.
    How to use: File → Recent files and select an entry. If there is no history it will appear disabled or empty.

  Web → Web measurements...
    What it is: dialog for querying or downloading measurements from the configured web service.
    What it does: incorporate server-stored spectra into the analysis.
    When to use: if the laboratory shares measurements via web or you are working with centralised data.
    How to use: File → Web → Web measurements..., choose the measurement and load it; then visually verify it matches the expected sample.

  Web → Web calibrations...
    What it is: access to calibrations available on the server.
    What it does: retrieve a calibration spectrum or parameters associated with a common calibration.
    When to use: before analysing samples measured under the same instrumental configuration.
    How to use: select the web calibration, load it and use it to adjust Vmax/centre or as an experimental reference.

  Web → Upload session JSON to web...
    What it is: sends the saved session of the current analysis to the server.
    What it does: share the complete fit state with other users: data, parameters, components and configuration.
    When to use: after completing a reproducible fit or when you want to review the result from another computer.
    How to use: first save or prepare a valid session; then use File → Web → Upload session JSON to web...
    Why it matters: uploading the session preserves much more context than uploading only the spectrum or an image.

  Use current file as calibration...
    What it is: treats the loaded spectrum as a calibration pattern.
    What it does: estimate or verify the velocity scale, especially Vmax and the centre, from a known calibrant.
    When to use: with standards such as α-Fe or another reference measured under the same instrumental conditions.
    How to use: load the standard, select this option and follow the dialog; then apply the calibration to the corresponding samples.
    Why it matters: an incorrect velocity scale shifts BHF, δ and ΔEQ, and can lead to a wrong physical interpretation.

  Save fit...
    What it is: saves numerical results of the current fit.
    What it does: store fitted parameters, metrics and components for documentation or external processing.
    When to use: after obtaining an acceptable RMS/residual and before modifying the model.
    How to use: run a fit, check the status panel and use File → Save fit...

  Export Markdown/PDF report...
    What it is: generates a readable report with a plot, parameters and analysis summary.
    What it does: deliver results, archive them or include them in laboratory reports.
    When to use: when finishing an analysis or when comparing several model hypotheses.
    How to use: fit the model, verify residuals and statistics, then choose the output format and path.

  Export interactive HTML plot...
    What it is: saves an interactive Plotly version of the plot.
    What it does: share a figure that allows zoom, point inspection and comfortable viewing outside the program.
    When to use: for discussion with other users or detailed review of specific spectral regions.
    How to use: when the Plotly tab is available and a spectrum is loaded, use File → Export interactive HTML plot...

  Save session...
    What it is: saves the complete program state to JSON.
    What it does: recover the analysis exactly as it was: data, parameters, components, mode and options.
    When to use: before closing, before trying an alternative model, or at the end of a working stage.
    How to use: File → Save session... or Ctrl+S; use names that indicate sample, model and date.

  Load session...
    What it is: restores a previously saved JSON session.
    What it does: continue a fit, review results or reproduce an analysis.
    When to use: when resuming work or opening a session shared by another person.
    How to use: File → Load session... or Ctrl+L; after loading, check that data paths and visual configuration are as expected.

  Exit
    Closes the application. Save the session before exiting if you have made important changes.

title: Files and web
heading: Data loading and web access
content:
Supported input files:

  • Modern WS5 files, XML files with a <data> block.
  • Older ADT files, plain count lists without XML header.
  • NORMOS sidecar files (.RES, .PLT, .JOB) are partially read when present.

Main file actions:

  Load...
    Opens local spectra. If a NORMOS .RES file is found next to the spectrum, the final folding point and starting parameters are imported when possible.

  Web measurements...
    Lists and downloads measurements through the laboratory REST API. Credentials are used to obtain a token; the password is not stored.

  Web calibrations...
    Lists and downloads α-Fe calibrations. If a measurement has an associated calibration, the calibrated Vmax can be applied.

  Use current file as calibration...
    Marks the currently loaded spectrum as a local calibration, without needing to
    download it from the web. Useful when you open a .ws5 or .adt file that contains
    an α-Fe or other velocity standard pattern.
    The dialog asks for:
      • Sample: name of the standard (pre-filled with the file name).
      • Vmax (mm/s): if provided, applied immediately to the active spectrum.
      • IS (mm/s): isomer shift of the standard (informational only).
    On confirmation the program records the calibration with source "local" (as opposed
    to "web_api" for web-sourced calibrations). The information is saved in the JSON
    session and appears in the info panel and exported report.

  Save fit...
    Exports a text table with velocity, normalized data, model, residual and folded counts. In distribution mode it also exports P(BHF) or P(ΔEQ).

  Save session...
    Stores the complete state in JSON: data, parameters, fixed/free flags, components, covariance, errors, constraints and calibration traceability.

  Export Markdown/PDF report...
    Creates a human-readable report with parameters, statistics, residual diagnostics, areas, correlations and calibration information. The Markdown file is always kept, even if a PDF is also generated.

title: Folding
heading: Folding, velocity axis and background
content:
The folding point is the internal symmetry center used to fold the two halves of the spectrum. Fractional values are supported, as in NORMOS.

Important parameters:

  Vmax
    Maximum velocity of the transducer, used to build the -Vmax ... +Vmax velocity axis. It should normally come from an α-Fe calibration. Signed Vmax values are allowed: a negative Vmax is kept negative and reverses the velocity-axis direction, matching web calibrations or NORMOS files that carry a signed velocity.

  Signed velocities and edge trimming
    The modular GUI discards the first and last points of the folded spectrum because these edge channels are often less reliable. After removing those two points the program does not rebuild a shorter axis from -Vmax to +Vmax, because that would stretch the velocity scale and bias BHF. Instead it builds the full NORMOS-like axis first and trims the same positions in both data and velocity:

      full_velocity = linspace(-Vmax, +Vmax, N)
      used_data     = full_data[1:-1]
      used_velocity = full_velocity[1:-1]

    If Vmax is fitted, this already-trimmed axis is scaled; it is not rebuilt with false end points.

  Fit Vmax
    Includes Vmax as a free parameter. This can compensate small calibration errors, but BHF and Vmax are strongly correlated, so BHF should normally be fixed when Vmax is fitted.

  Folding point
    Internal channel center. The GUI also displays an approximate NORMOS folding value.

  Fit folding point
    Includes the folding center as a free parameter in discrete fits. At each iteration the counts are refolded and statistical weights are recomputed. Use it only near a reasonable starting value and with a limited number of free parameters.

  Baseline and slope
    Model the normalized transmission background. Do not use a large slope to hide a wrong folding point.

A wrong folding point often produces antisymmetric positive-negative residual pairs around the absorption lines.

title: Fit menu
heading: Fit menu: running, preparing and controlling the physical model
content:
The Fit menu is the centre of quantitative analysis. It runs the fit, prepares initial values, selects the model type and activates statistical or physical options that change how parameters are optimised.

General rule before using this menu:

  1. Load the spectrum.
  2. Check the folding point, Vmax, baseline and slope.
  3. Choose the model: discrete or P(BHF)/P(ΔEQ) distribution.
  4. Fix parameters you know physically and release only those you want to estimate.
  5. Run the fit and review residuals, reduced χ², AIC/BIC and correlations.

The menu is organised into: Fit, Fit mode (seven modes), Preparation (centre, minima and AI), Error analysis (bootstrap, profile-likelihood and L-curve), Batch fit, parameter management (release/fix, constraints, presets) and Advanced options.

Menu subitems:

  Fit
    What it is: runs the optimisation with the current free parameters.
    What it does: find the values of δ, ΔEQ, BHF, widths, intensities, depths or distribution weights that best reproduce the spectrum.
    When to use: when you already have a reasonable initial model; do not use it blindly as a first step.
    How to use: Fit → Fit or Ctrl+R; inspect the residual and the metrics.
    Why: numerical fitting only converges to useful solutions if it starts from physically plausible parameters.

  Fit mode → Crystalline fit
    Discrete model mode with sharp components: singlets, doublets or sextets.
    Use to represent well-defined phases with specific hyperfine parameters.

  Fit mode → P(BHF) distribution
    Hyperfine field distribution mode, and entry point to P(ΔEQ) from the distribution tab.
    Use to describe broadening produced by a continuum of fields or local environments.

  Preparation → Find centre
    Estimates the folding point or spectrum centre.
    Use immediately after loading a measurement if symmetric lines are not well aligned.
    How to use: Fit → Preparation → Find centre; then visually inspect the symmetry of the folded spectrum.
    Why: a misplaced centre can mimic changes in δ, worsen the residual and distort areas.

  Preparation → Initialise from minima
    Detects absorption minima and proposes initial parameters.
    Use for spectra with relatively well-defined sextets/doublets and good signal-to-noise ratio.
    How to use: load the spectrum, roughly adjust baseline/Vmax, activate the components and run; review the proposed values manually.

  Preparation → Edit minima (semi-manual)...
    Interactive editor of the detected minima before using them for initialisation.
    Use after Initialise from minima if the automatic proposal does not reflect the real lines.

  Preparation → Auto-fit from minima
    Combines initialisation from minima with a first automatic optimisation.
    Use as an exploratory starting point for routine measurements or clear spectra.

  Preparation → Local AI Ollama: suggest start...
    Requests a model initialisation suggestion from a local AI.
    Use when Ollama is installed/configured and you want a quick second opinion.
    Only apply what is physically reasonable; the AI can orient but not validate.

  Error analysis → Bootstrap errors (MC)...
    Estimates uncertainties by resampling the spectrum using Monte Carlo.
    Use after an already stable and acceptable discrete fit.
    Why: the local covariance matrix may underestimate uncertainties if there are non-parabolic χ² valleys.

  Error analysis → Profile-likelihood errors...
    Explores how fit quality changes when a parameter is forced to different values while the rest re-optimise.
    Use when an important parameter has a doubtful error, high correlations or asymmetric limits.
    Why: reveals the real sensitivity of the spectrum to that parameter, not just a local linear approximation.

  Error analysis → L-curve α
    Regularisation scan for P(BHF)/P(ΔEQ) distributions.
    Use whenever a distribution depends on α and you intend to interpret the shape physically.

  Batch fit...
    Applies fits to several spectra sequentially.
    Use when there are many similar files and reproducibility is required.

  Release all
    Unchecks all "fixed" controls. Use only in final stages or when the model is already close to the solution.
    Why: releasing too many parameters early favours instability and correlations.

  Fix all
    Checks all "fixed" controls. Use when building a fit step by step.
    Why: controlling the degrees of freedom improves convergence and makes interpretation clearer.

  Parameter constraints...
    Opens the editor for linear relations between parameters.
    Use to link parameters that must be equal or proportional for physical reasons.

  Physical constraint presets...
    Collection of common constraints ready to apply.
    Use when preparing standard models with theoretical intensities, equal widths or linked parameters.

  Advanced fit options → Line profile → Lorentzian
    Classical Mössbauer line profile. Default choice for most fits. Use when lines are well described by conventional symmetric profiles.

  Advanced fit options → Line profile → Voigt
    Convolution of Lorentzian with a Gaussian contribution. Use if lines show tails or broadening that a Lorentzian cannot describe well.

  Advanced fit options → Likelihood → Gauss (χ², data σ)
    Weighted least-squares with uncertainty estimated from the data. Default for spectra with good statistics.

  Advanced fit options → Likelihood → Poisson (IRLS, model σ)
    Iterative fit adapted to Poisson counts. Use for low counts or noticeable heteroscedasticity.

  Advanced fit options → Robust loss → Linear
    Ordinary least squares without robustification. Default when there are no spurious spikes, damaged channels or outliers.

  Advanced fit options → Robust loss → Soft L1
    Smoothly reduces the weight of large residuals. Use if there are a few anomalous channels or non-ideal noise.

  Advanced fit options → Robust loss → Huber
    Quadratic for small residuals and approximately linear for large ones. Use if you suspect problematic channels.

  Advanced fit options → Propagate calibration σ (vmax)
    Includes the velocity calibration uncertainty in error propagation.
    Use when the calibration has relevant uncertainty or when final errors are to be reported.

  Advanced fit options → Global optimisation (DE) before TRF
    Uses differential evolution to search for a global solution before local TRF refinement.
    Use with many free parameters, overlapping components or difficult distributions.

  Advanced fit options → Absorber → Thin (linear)
    Thin-absorber model, linear in absorption. Default for sufficiently thin samples.

  Advanced fit options → Absorber → Thick (saturation)
    Model that accounts for absorber thickness/saturation effects.
    Use if the sample is thick or if relative intensities do not match despite a reasonable physical model.
    Saturation is noticeable when absorption exceeds ~15–20% of the baseline level.
    Model: T(v) = b + s·v − C·(1 − exp(−A_total(v)/C))
    C is the saturation scale; C→∞ recovers the thin model.
    Reference: Margulies, S. & Ehrman, J.R. (1961). Nucl. Instrum. Methods 12, 131–137.
    Full implementation details: docs/correccion_espesor.pdf

  Advanced fit options → P(BHF): add active sharp components
    Adds the active discrete components to the distribution model.
    Use if the spectrum shows a broad continuous part and also well-defined narrow lines.

title: Discrete model
heading: Singlets, doublets and sextets
content:
Each component tab can be configured as a singlet, doublet or sextet.

  Singlet
    One absorption line. Main parameters: δ, Γ, depth and intensity.

  Doublet
    Two lines separated by ΔEQ. Useful for paramagnetic Fe sites with quadrupole interaction.

  Sextet
    Six magnetic lines. Main parameters: δ, ΔEQ, BHF, Γ, depth and relative intensities. The default starting values are depth = 0.02 for component 1 and Γ = 0.15 mm/s. The depth slider is limited to 0–0.07 for easier manual modelling, but the internal fit bound remains wider.

  NORMOS-style intensities
    int3 is hidden and internally fixed to 1: lines 3&4 are the implicit reference. The visible controls are int1≈D13 and int2≈D23. For a random powder use int1=3, int2=2, int3=1; depth/DEP is the global absorption scale.

Line profile (Options ▸ Line profile):

  Lorentzian
    Default profile. Appropriate for natural or homogeneous broadening.

  Voigt
    Convolution of a Lorentzian (width γ) with a Gaussian of σ = {voigt_sigma:.3g} mm/s. Useful for additional instrumental or static broadening. Areas are computed by numerical integration, so Lorentzian and Voigt profiles are treated consistently.

  σ is the global Gaussian width and only applies to the Voigt profile. The Fit σ checkbox next to the σ slider in Velocity, folding and background lets σ be a free parameter of the fit alongside vmax and the folding point; it is active only in Voigt mode and is unchecked by default. Right-click the σ slider/box to switch quickly between Lorentzian and Voigt. Mind the σ–γ correlation, which can inflate the error bars if the data do not constrain the line shape well.

The Fit button optimizes all non-fixed parameters. The status panel reports RMS, χ²red, AIC, BIC, areas, errors and high correlations when available.

title: Magnetic relaxation
heading: Magnetic / superparamagnetic relaxation
content:
The program includes two component types for magnetic relaxation in nanoparticles or superparamagnetic materials. They are additional to singlet, doublet, sextet and P(BHF); existing models are not changed unless these component types are used.

Relajacion is a phenomenological mixture of a blocked sextet and an apparent superparamagnetic doublet. Its specific parameters are blocked fraction and log10 ν (s⁻¹). The effective blocked fraction is conceptually f_blocked,eff = f_blocked · f_dynamic(log10ν), so f_blocked and log10ν can be strongly correlated. Practical rule: free f_blocked with log10ν fixed to estimate fractions; free log10ν with f_blocked fixed to 1 to study dynamics; avoid freeing both unless the spectrum clearly constrains them.

BlumeTjon is a first dynamic two-state model +BHF ↔ -BHF. It has no explicit blocked fraction; log10ν controls the exchange. Slow rates tend to a sextet, intermediate rates broaden/coalesce lines, and fast rates tend to an averaged doublet/singlet.

Interpretation warning: an apparent doublet together with a sextet may be fast relaxation, not necessarily a separate paramagnetic phase. A broad low-field P(BHF) may also be absorbing dynamic effects.


NeelSize adds a Néel–Arrhenius size-distribution component. A lognormal diameter distribution is converted to volumes V = πd³/6 and rates ν(d,T)=1/τ0 exp[-Keff V/(kB T)]. The total spectrum is a weighted sum of BlumeTjon spectra over size bins. Parameters are T, log10 Keff, d50, lognormal σ, log10 τ0 and number of size bins. Large particles tend to be blocked, small ones relax fast and intermediate ones partially collapse.

A pure backend for global multi-temperature fitting is available: it shares the physical NeelSize parameters across spectra and keeps baseline, slope and depth local. This is the recommended way to estimate Keff and size distributions, because a single spectrum rarely identifies all parameters.

Current status / TODO: this global multi-temperature fit does NOT yet have its own GUI dialog. In the graphical interface, NeelSize is currently used as a single-spectrum component; GUI integration for selecting several spectra, assigning temperatures and launching the global fit remains pending.

References:

  Blume-Tjon two-state model:
    Blume, M. & Tjon, J.A. (1968). Mössbauer spectra in a fluctuating environment.
    Phys. Rev. 165, 446–456.

  Néel-Arrhenius relaxation:
    Néel, L. (1949). Théorie du traînage magnétique des ferromagnétiques en grains
    fins avec applications aux terres cuites. Ann. Géophys. 5, 99–136.
    Brown, W.F. (1963). Thermal fluctuations of a single-domain particle.
    Phys. Rev. 130, 1677–1686.

  Full mathematics of the implementation: docs/relajacion_mossbauer.pdf

title: Line profile
heading: Lorentzian and Voigt
content:
The program supports two line profiles (Fit ▸ Advanced options ▸ Line profile):

  Lorentzian (default profile)
    The natural profile of a nuclear transition without inhomogeneous broadening. The standard choice for most Mössbauer fits. Longer tails than a Gaussian.

  Voigt
    Convolution of a Lorentzian (natural width γ) with a Gaussian (inhomogeneous broadening of width σ). Useful when a distribution of local environments broadens the lines symmetrically without requiring a full P(BHF) distribution.

  The σ parameter is the width of the Gaussian component of the Voigt (σ slider in the Calibration box) and only applies with the Voigt profile. The Fit σ checkbox, next to the σ slider in Velocity, folding and background, allows σ to be a free fit parameter alongside vmax and the folding point; it is only active in Voigt mode and is unchecked by default. Be aware of the σ–γ correlation, which can widen error bars if the data do not pin the line shape well.

  The profile can also be changed by right-clicking on the σ slider/box: a context menu appears to switch between Lorentzian ↔ Voigt without opening the menu.

When to use Voigt:

  • Lines are clearly broader than expected but maintain a symmetric shape.
  • Moderate inhomogeneous broadening is suspected (nanocrystals, partial substitutions).
  • The Lorentzian fit leaves residuals in the line wings.

When to use P(BHF) instead:

  • The broadening is asymmetric or has structure (shoulders, secondary peaks).
  • A real distribution of hyperfine fields is suspected (magnetic disorder, particle size).
  • The Voigt does not sufficiently improve the residual.

title: Constraints
heading: Linear constraints and physical presets
content:
Linear constraints impose:

  target = factor · source + offset

The target parameter is removed from the optimizer and updated from the source at each model evaluation.

Typical uses:

  • Equal line widths between components.
  • Equal isomer shifts.
  • Fixed intensity ratios.
  • Reduced number of free parameters when correlations are high.

Physical presets:

  Fit → Physical constraint presets...

They can quickly impose powder sextet intensities 3:2:1, equal widths within components, equal δ between active components, or equal Γ1 between active components.

title: P(BHF)
heading: Hyperfine field distribution P(BHF)
content:
Distribution mode represents the spectrum as a sum of many sextets with different hyperfine fields. The result P(BHF) is the spectral weight associated with each BHF value.

Method:

  • Define a BHF grid between B min and B max.
  • Each grid point contributes a sextet.
  • Weights are constrained to be non-negative.
  • The second difference of P(BHF) is penalized to avoid non-physical oscillations.

The minimized expression is schematically:

  weighted spectral residual² + α · roughness(P)²

The residual is weighted using Poisson uncertainties estimated from the folded counts. Therefore channels with higher statistical noise contribute less to the cost.

Alternative regularization:

  Besides Tikhonov (second-difference, smooth), the distribution panel lets you
  choose Total Variation (TV), which penalizes the total variation of P instead of
  its curvature. TV is better at preserving sharp edges in P(BHF) at the cost of
  slightly more noise sensitivity in flat regions.

Uncertainty band and effective degrees of freedom:

  A 1σ uncertainty band around P(BHF) is drawn automatically from the linearised
  weight covariance (clipped to P≥0). The reduced χ² uses effective degrees of
  freedom (trace of the hat matrix) rather than the raw bin count.
  See chapter "Accessing options & new features" for details.

Reference:

  Hesse, J. & Rübartsch, A. (1974). Model independent evaluation of overlapped
    Mössbauer spectra. J. Phys. E: Sci. Instrum. 7, 526–532.
  Full mathematics: docs/ajuste_mossbauer.pdf

title: P(BHF): method
heading: Hesse-Rübartsch method and regularization
content:
The engine implements a Hesse-Rübartsch approach (standard method in Mössbauer spectroscopy):

Algorithm steps:

  1. A uniform grid of N_bins fields between B_min and B_max is defined.
  2. For each bin the corresponding sextet is computed (response matrix A).
  3. Weights P ≥ 0 are sought that minimise:

     residual² + α · ||D² P||²

  4. D² is the second-difference matrix: it penalises the curvature of P(BHF).
  5. The problem is solved iteratively with a non-negativity constraint.

Objective function:

  χ² = ||y − A·P||² + α · ||D²·P||²

  The first term measures the fit to the data. The second measures the roughness of P(BHF).

Role of the parameter α:

  α = 0        The fit reproduces the spectrum maximally, but P(BHF) fills with spurious peaks.
  α small      Detailed P(BHF) with possible noise oscillations.
  α optimal    Compromise between data fidelity and physical smoothness.
  α large      Very smooth P(BHF), possibly too smooth, losing real structure.

  The optimal value is not universal: it depends on the signal-to-noise ratio, the number of channels, the number of bins and the line width. Always explore several α values and verify that the main features of P(BHF) are stable.

Available P(BHF) shapes:

  Histogram (free, non-parametric)
    Each bin has an independently adjustable weight. Maximum flexibility, more sensitive to the choice of α. The most commonly used shape.

  Gaussian
    The distribution is parameterised as a Gaussian (centre, width, amplitude). Reduces the degrees of freedom to 3. Useful when a well-defined unimodal distribution is expected. Does not require α.

  Binomial
    Combinatorial model for ferrites with cationic substitutions. The shape of P(BHF) follows the binomial distribution of substituents in first-neighbour shells. Fits only substitution probability and amplitude. Does not require α.

  Fixed
    Loads an externally defined P(BHF) (file with two columns: centre, weight) and fits only the global amplitude, baseline and slope. Useful for comparison with published distributions or to refine δ, ΔEQ and Γ while keeping the shape fixed.

  All four shapes support sharp components (by enabling "add active sharp components"). Sharp components are fitted simultaneously with the distribution in all cases.

Alternative regularisation: Total Variation (TV):

  In addition to Tikhonov (second differences), the distribution panel allows
  Total Variation (TV) to be selected. TV penalises the total variation of P(BHF)
  instead of its curvature: it preserves sharp edges and narrow peaks better at the
  cost of greater noise sensitivity in smooth regions.

1σ band and effective degrees of freedom:

  After fitting, a 1σ uncertainty band around P(BHF) is drawn automatically,
  calculated from the linearised weight covariance (clipped to P≥0).
  Reduced χ² uses effective degrees of freedom (trace of the hat matrix)
  rather than the raw bin count, to avoid overestimating fit quality.
  For more detail: chapter "Accessing options & new features".

References:

  Hesse, J. & Rübartsch, A. (1974). Model independent evaluation of overlapped
    Mössbauer spectra. J. Phys. E: Sci. Instrum. 7, 526–532.
  Full mathematical documentation: docs/ajuste_mossbauer.pdf

title: P(BHF): parameters
heading: P(BHF) distribution parameters
content:
Global parameters (common to all sextets in the distribution):

  Global δ
    Common isomer shift. If the distribution contains phases with very different δ, use sharp components for each phase instead.

  Global ΔEQ
    Common quadrupole (first-order correction). In P(BHF) it is refined if its "fixed" checkbox is unchecked; in P(ΔEQ) it is the distributed variable and does not act as a global parameter.

  Γ FWHM
    Line width common to all bins. A larger Γ smooths the distribution and reduces instability but obscures fine structure. A smaller Γ can better reproduce narrow peaks but requires a larger α to stabilise.

Grid parameters:

  B min
    Lower limit of the distribution. B_min = 0 allows low-field contributions (paramagnetic phases, relaxation) to be absorbed, but may introduce artificial weight if there is no real signal there. For purely magnetic phases, starting at 10–20 T is advisable.

  B max
    Upper limit. Must exceed the maximum expected BHF. For haematite (~52 T) B_max ≥ 55 T is recommended. For α-Fe (~33 T) 36–38 T suffices.

  BHF bins
    Number of grid points. More bins = higher potential resolution + greater instability. For typical 512-channel spectra, 30–60 bins is usually sufficient. With many bins and low α, artefact oscillations may appear.

Regularisation control:

  log10 α
    Logarithm of the regularisation parameter. A value between −2 and 0 is typical. Vary in steps of 0.5 and observe the stability of P(BHF) and RMS.

  L-curve α
    The "L-curve α" button automatically scans a wide range of α values and opens a window with the L-curve (log residual vs. log roughness) and RMS together with reduced χ² versus α. It proposes three values: the L-curve corner (maximum curvature), a "compromise" value, and the GCV (generalised cross-validation) criterion. It is a starting point, not a definitive answer: verify the stability of P(BHF) around the chosen value.
    Reference: Hansen, P.C. (1992). Analysis of discrete ill-posed problems by means of the L-curve. SIAM Rev. 34, 561–580.

  Fixed distribution
    Loads an external P(BHF) (two columns: BHF, weight) and applies it without fitting the weights. Useful for comparison with reference results or to refine only δ, Γ with a known distribution.

title: P(BHF): sharp components
heading: Sharp components alongside the distribution
content:
It is possible to combine a continuous P(BHF) or P(ΔEQ) distribution with one or more discrete ("sharp") components in the same fit. Sharp components work with all distribution shapes: Histogram, Gaussian, Binomial and Fixed.

How to activate:

  Check the option "add active sharp components" in the Distribution tab.

  When activated, component 1 is always used as a sharp component. Components 2 and 3 are used if they are enabled ("Use component 2/3" checked in their tab). Their parameters (δ, ΔEQ, BHF, Γ, intensities) are taken from the sliders in each component tab.

When it is useful:

  • A sample has a disordered majority phase (broad P(BHF)) and a well-defined minority phase (narrow sextet).
  • Example: zinc ferrite with a broad distribution + a trace of α-Fe at BHF ≈ 33 T.
  • Example: amorphous Fe oxide (broad P(BHF)) + a paramagnetic Fe²⁺ doublet.
  • Example: glass with broad P(ΔEQ) + a metallic Fe singlet.

How it works internally:

  • The distribution weights are fitted as usual (with regularisation for Histogram, parametrically for Gaussian/Binomial, with global amplitude for Fixed).
  • The sharp components are added as additional columns to the equation system.
  • Their amplitudes are fitted together with the distribution weights, with a non-negativity constraint but without regularisation.
  • The Status panel shows the area percentages of the distribution and of each sharp component separately.

Differences between shapes:

  Histogram
    Maximum flexibility for the distribution. Sharp components only compete with distribution bins if they overlap in the spectrum. The most commonly used combination.

  Gaussian / Binomial
    The distribution has few parameters and a rigid shape. Sharp components are especially useful here because they absorb phases that the fixed distribution shape cannot reproduce.

  Fixed
    The distribution shape is predefined. Sharp components add contributions not included in the external distribution.

Precautions:

  • A sharp component may compete with a P(BHF) peak if its BHF falls within the distribution range. The fit may distribute the signal arbitrarily between them.
  • If the sharp component has a known BHF (e.g. α-Fe at 33 T as a calibrant), fix it.
  • Compare fits with and without the sharp component to verify that it brings a real statistical improvement.
  • Increasing α may transfer weight from the sharp component to the distribution or vice versa.
  • If a sharp component comes out with weight ≈ 0, it is probably not needed in the model.

title: P(ΔEQ)
heading: Quadrupole-splitting distribution P(ΔEQ)
content:
P(ΔEQ) is the quadrupolar analogue of P(BHF): instead of distributing hyperfine
fields it distributes quadrupole splitting values ΔEQ, keeping a single fixed BHF
shared by all contributions.

Model:

  spectrum = background − Σᵢ Pᵢ · sextet/doublet(BHF_fixed, ΔEQᵢ, δ, Γ)

  If BHF_fixed = 0 each term is a doublet; otherwise a sextet with varying ΔEQ.

Typical uses:

  • Disordered paramagnetic Fe environments: Fe²⁺ or Fe³⁺ in glasses, zeolites,
    clays or amorphous materials where each Fe site has a different electric field
    gradient.
  • Ferritin-like or amorphous iron-oxide phases.
  • Superparamagnetic samples above the blocking temperature, where the spectrum
    collapses to a broad doublet reflecting a real ΔEQ distribution.
  • Samples where a single doublet is too simple but a magnetic distribution is
    not appropriate.

Key parameters:

  Fixed BHF for P(ΔEQ)
    Magnetic field shared by all sextet bins. Set to 0 for purely paramagnetic
    doublets. Set to a non-zero value (e.g. 33 T for α-Fe-like environments) to
    model a magnetically-split phase whose quadrupole interaction varies.

  ΔEQ min / max (mm/s)
    Grid limits. For paramagnetic Fe³⁺ typically 0–2 mm/s; for distorted Fe²⁺
    possibly 0–4 mm/s.

  δ global
    Single isomer shift for all bins. If both Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ are present use sharp
    components for each species rather than a single P(ΔEQ).

  α, bins, Γ
    Work identically to the P(BHF) case. See chapter P(BHF): method.

Specific warnings:

  • With BHF_fixed ≠ 0 the ΔEQ distribution may correlate with δ: a shift of the
    whole P(ΔEQ) can mimic a change in δ. Fix δ when it is known.
  • If the spectrum has both a BHF distribution and a ΔEQ distribution, neither
    1D mode is fully satisfactory. Consider P(BHF,ΔEQ) 2D as an exploratory model.
  • All four distribution shapes (Histogram, Gaussian, Binomial, Fixed) are
    available and work identically to P(BHF), including sharp components.

title: P(BHF, ΔEQ): 2D distribution
content:
The two-dimensional P(BHF, ΔEQ) distribution is an advanced model for magnetic populations where both hyperfine field and quadrupole splitting vary. An initial backend is available in mossbauer_distribution.fit_bhf_quad_distribution() and an initial GUI integration is available as the "2D BHF-ΔEQ" distribution shape. After fitting, a heat map with P(BHF) and P(ΔEQ) marginals is shown.

It minimizes the residual plus smoothness penalties in both directions:

  ||y - Xp||² + αB ||D²B P||² + αQ ||D²Q P||²,  P ≥ 0

Important warning: this model can fit very well and still be physically meaningless. A 30×30 grid already has 900 weights for only a few hundred channels. With too little regularization the 2D map may absorb noise, folding/calibration errors, wrong linewidths or missing components. A nice 2D map is not proof of a real field-quadrupole distribution. Check stability versus αB, αQ and binning, inspect residuals, compare with simpler P(BHF), P(ΔEQ) or discrete models, and use external information whenever possible.


Current GUI features:

  • Main "P(BHF, ΔEQ) 2D" mode and "2D BHF-ΔEQ" distribution shape.
  • Heat map with P(BHF) and P(ΔEQ) marginals.
  • Interactive Plotly heat map.
  • TSV export of the full map.
  • αB/αQ L-surface to explore 2D regularization.
  • Simultaneous sharp components, with free or fixed depths.
  • Markdown/PDF report with diagnostics and 2D map.

Reported diagnostics include apparent mean and sigma of BHF and ΔEQ, apparent corr(BHF,ΔEQ), approximate effective degrees of freedom and warnings when the grid has more weights than channels or the effective dof is high. These numbers describe the fitted map; they are not independent proof of physical reality.


IS extension:

  In addition to P(BHF, ΔEQ), the GUI includes 1D P(IS), 2D P(IS, ΔEQ) and 2D P(BHF, IS). P(IS, ΔEQ) is aimed at broad paramagnetic doublets and amorphous materials where isomer shift and quadrupole splitting vary together. P(BHF, IS) can explore field-shift correlations, but it is especially sensitive to velocity calibration and folding.

  Warning: IS correlates with folding, Vmax, calibration offset, QS and Γ. A smooth nice-looking P(IS) or P(IS,QS) may simply compensate a wrong velocity scale or an incomplete background/model. Fix calibration whenever possible and compare with simpler models.

title: P(IS) and P(IS, ΔEQ)
content:
These distributions describe continuous variation of the isomer shift IS/δ.

1D P(IS):

  T(v) = background - Σᵢ P(ISᵢ) · S(v; ISᵢ, QS, BHF, Γ)

Typical use: families of sites with slightly different isomer shifts, amorphous materials, glasses, silicates, ferritins or samples with valence/coordination distributions. If BHF = 0, each point contributes as a paramagnetic doublet/singlet depending on QS.

2D P(IS, ΔEQ): useful for broad paramagnetic doublets where both the doublet center and splitting vary. Approximate line positions are v₁ = IS - ΔEQ/2 and v₂ = IS + ΔEQ/2.

2D P(BHF, IS): explores field-shift correlations in disordered magnetic phases, surfaces or size/oxidation distributions.

Important warning: IS strongly correlates with folding point, Vmax, isomer-shift reference, QS, Γ, background and missing components. A smooth P(IS) or P(IS,QS) with good RMS may compensate a bad calibration or incomplete model. Verify velocity calibration, check folding, compare with discrete and P(ΔEQ) models, vary αIS/αQS and binning, and only interpret stable features.

Full mathematics: docs/distribuciones_is_mossbauer.pdf.

title: L-curve
heading: Choosing the regularization parameter α
content:
The L-curve button scans a range of α values and opens a window with:

  • log residual norm versus log roughness.
  • RMS and reduced χ² versus α.
  • the maximum-curvature L-curve corner suggestion.
  • an additional compromise suggestion.
  • a GCV (generalised cross-validation) suggestion.
  • a button to save the complete scan table.

Small α values may overfit noise and create spurious peaks. Large α values oversmooth the distribution and may hide real structure. The L-curve is a guide, not an automatic proof.

Reference:

  Hansen, P.C. (1992). Analysis of discrete ill-posed problems by means of the
  L-curve. SIAM Rev. 34, 561–580.

title: Statistics
heading: Weights, model selection and robust errors
content:
Poisson weights:

  The fit uses uncertainties estimated from folded counts. The minimized residual is (model - data) / σ.

Reduced χ², AIC and BIC:

  Reduced χ² measures weighted residuals per degree of freedom. AIC and BIC penalize the number of free parameters and help compare models fitted to the same data.

Numerical areas:

  Areas are obtained by integrating the actual component profile on the velocity axis. This is valid for both Lorentzian and Voigt profiles.

Correlations:

  High |r| values in the covariance matrix mean that two parameters are not independently determined. Fixing one parameter, adding a physically justified constraint or simplifying the model may be better than adding more freedom.

Bootstrap Monte Carlo:

  Fit → Error analysis → Bootstrap errors (MC)... generates synthetic spectra by adding noise to the fitted model and refits each replica. The standard deviation of fitted parameters gives a more robust uncertainty estimate for discrete models.

title: Diagnostics
heading: Residuals and common problems
content:
The residual, data minus model, should look like random statistical noise.

Common patterns:

  • Antisymmetric positive-negative pairs around lines: wrong folding point.
  • Curved or sloped background: wrong Vmax, normalization problem or real detector trend.
  • Model lines broader than data: Γ too large.
  • Residual peaks in the line wings: consider Voigt profile or a distribution.
  • Structure in distribution mode: α may be too large or the model may be incomplete.
  • High-frequency oscillations in P(BHF): α is probably too small.

The status panel reports lag-1 autocorrelation, a runs-test z value and an antisymmetric residual indicator.

title: Saving and reports
heading: Saving results, sessions and reports
content:
Save fit (.dat):

  Exports velocity, normalized data, model, residual and folded counts. Distribution fits include the distribution table.

Save session (.json):

  Saves the full working state, including parameters, fixed/free flags, components, covariance, errors, constraints and status text. If calibration metadata are available, they are stored too.

Export Markdown/PDF report:

  Creates a documented report with version, file, folding point, Vmax, calibration, metrics, areas, parameters, errors, correlations and residual diagnostics. If PDF export is selected, the Markdown file is still preserved.

Automatic settings are stored in:

  {settings_path_str}

title: Reference values
heading: Typical 57Fe reference parameters
content:
Room-temperature values relative to α-Fe, approximate only:

  α-Fe:          δ ≈ 0.00 mm/s, ΔEQ ≈ 0.00 mm/s, BHF ≈ 33.0 T.
  Fe3+ oct.:     δ ≈ 0.30-0.45 mm/s, ΔEQ ≈ 0.4-1.0 mm/s.
  Fe2+ oct.:     δ ≈ 1.0-1.3 mm/s, ΔEQ ≈ 2.0-3.5 mm/s.
  Magnetite A:   δ ≈ 0.28 mm/s, BHF ≈ 49 T.
  Magnetite B:   δ ≈ 0.65 mm/s, BHF ≈ 46 T.
  Hematite:      δ ≈ 0.37 mm/s, ΔEQ ≈ -0.2 mm/s, BHF ≈ 52 T.
  Goethite:      δ ≈ 0.37 mm/s, ΔEQ ≈ -0.25 mm/s, BHF ≈ 38 T.
  Ferritin:      δ ≈ 0.45-0.50 mm/s, ΔEQ ≈ 0.7-1.0 mm/s.

Temperature, substitutions, particle size and disorder may shift these values.

title: View menu
heading: View menu: plot display, themes, language and layout
content:
The View menu controls how information is presented. It does not change the mathematical result of the fit, except insofar as improved visualisation helps you diagnose the model better.

Menu subitems:

  Show difference
    Shows or hides the residual. Use it to see structure not explained by the model. If the residual is not random, check centre, Vmax, components, widths or constraints.

  Show legend
    Shows or hides the plot legend. Useful for identifying components during analysis; can be hidden when preparing screenshots or visual reports.

  Show interactive Plotly tab
    What it is: activates or opens the interactive Plotly visualisation when available.
    What it does: enables zoom, point inspection and exploration of curve details.
    When to use: for fine analysis of overlapping lines or when preparing a review with another person.
    How to use: select the option and work in the Plotly tab; if QtWebEngine is missing, use the HTML export.

  Visual theme
    Changes the general Qt style. Use for ergonomics; does not affect data or parameters.

  Plot style → Classic
    Simple and familiar style. Use for continuity with previous analyses.

  Plot style → Modern
    Cleaner style for daily work and internal presentations.

  Plot style → Publication
    Style designed for sober, readable figures in reports or articles.

  Plot style → Dark
    Plot style adapted to dark backgrounds. Use if you work with a dark theme or in presentations with a dark background.

  Colour theme
    Changes the interface palette. Use to improve contrast or visual comfort. Does not affect calculations.

  Language
    What it is: application language selector.
    What it does: change the text of menus, help and controls available in the translation files.
    When to use: if you prefer to work in Spanish, English or French.
    How to use: View → Language → choose language; some text may require a restart to apply fully.

  Configure panel layout...
    What it is: opens the panel layout configurator.
    What it does: rearrange the header, file, info, calibration, reference and simulation/fit control panels.
    When to use: on small screens, wide monitors, projectors or workflows where you want to prioritise the plot or parameters.
    How to use: select a preset or distribution and confirm; save the session if you want to preserve the environment.
    Why: a comfortable layout reduces errors when fitting many parameters.

title: Help menu
heading: Help menu: documentation, version, changelog and updates
content:
The Help menu brings together documentation and program maintenance.

Menu subitems:

  Help
    What it is: opens this help window.
    What it does: consult usage chapters, fundamentals and menu descriptions.
    When to use: when learning the program, reviewing a specific option or documenting a procedure.
    How to use: Help → Help or F1; select a chapter from the side list.

  About
    What it is: shows version information and general application details.
    What it does: check which version is installed when reporting bugs or comparing results.
    When to use: before sending a report, updating or verifying that the expected version is in use.
    How to use: Help → About.

  Changelog
    What it is: technical list of changes by version.
    What it does: see what functions were added, fixed or changed.
    When to use: after updating, when reproducing old analyses, or when checking whether an improvement already exists.
    How to use: Help → Changelog; review the relevant version.

  Check for updates...
    What it is: checks whether a new version is available according to the update configuration.
    What it does: keep the program up to date.
    When to use: if you suspect there is a newer version, before reporting a bug, or periodically.
    How to use: Help → Check for updates...; if an update appears, follow the dialog instructions.
    Why: new versions may include numerical fixes, interface improvements and updated documentation.

  Configure updates...
    What it is: opens options related to the update channel or mechanism.
    What it does: decide how versions are checked and from which source.
    When to use: when installing the program, changing between stable/beta channels, or solving connection problems.
    How to use: Help → Configure updates..., adjust the options and save.
    Why: a correct configuration avoids incorrect alerts and helps maintain a reproducible environment.

title: Shortcuts and quick workflow
heading: Efficient workflow
content:
Main controls:

  "Fit" button
    Runs the fit with the current free parameters. In discrete mode uses least_squares; in distribution mode uses the engine for the selected shape.

  "Release all" button
    Unchecks all "fixed" checkboxes, allowing all parameters to be fitted.

  Fit menu → Fix all
    Checks all "fixed" checkboxes. Useful before selectively releasing only the desired parameters.

  Fit menu → Fit mode
    Switches between the discrete model and P(BHF)/P(ΔEQ) distribution.

  Fit menu → Constraints
    Opens the linear constraints dialog between parameters.

  Fit menu → Physical constraint presets
    Applies common physical relationships with one click (3:2:1 intensities, equal widths, link δ or Γ between components). See chapter "What is new".

  Fit menu → Error analysis → Bootstrap errors (MC)
    Estimates parameter errors by Monte Carlo resampling. Discrete mode only. See chapter "What is new".

  File menu → Export Markdown/PDF report
    Generates a readable report of the current fit. See chapter "Saving and reports".

  Help menu → Changelog
    Shows the complete list of changes for all program versions.

  Numerical entry boxes
    In addition to sliders, you can type an exact value in the box and press Enter to apply it.

Quick workflow for a known spectrum:

  1. Load the spectrum.
  2. Check that the folding point and Vmax are correct (verify that outer lines end at ≈ Vmax).
  3. Select the component type (Sextet for magnetic phases, Doublet for paramagnetic).
  4. Fix BHF to the expected value, release δ, ΔEQ and depth.
  5. Press Fit. Review the residual.
  6. If the residual is good, also release Γ.
  7. Fit again. Note the RMS.
  8. Save the session.

Workflow for a P(BHF) spectrum:

  1. Load and verify folding.
  2. In Fit → Fit mode, select "P(BHF) distribution" (or P(ΔEQ)).
  3. Estimate B_min, B_max, global δ, ΔEQ and Γ; release only those you want to refine.
  4. Start with log10 α = 0 (very smooth) and fit.
  5. Reduce α in steps of 0.5 until stable structure appears in P(BHF).
  6. Use L-curve α to guide the choice of optimal value.
  7. Compare P(BHF) at 3–4 α values to identify robust features.
  8. If there are discrete phases, activate "add active sharp components" and configure the components.
  9. Save the session for each significant α.

Workflow for Gaussian/Binomial P(BHF) with sharp components:

  1. Configure the distribution as above.
  2. Select Gaussian or Binomial shape.
  3. Activate "add active sharp components".
  4. Configure the sharp component parameters (e.g. paramagnetic singlet or doublet).
  5. Fit. The program simultaneously optimises the parametric distribution and the sharp component weights.
  6. Compare RMS with and without sharp components to verify that they bring improvement.

title: Accessing options & new features
heading: Recent options and how to reach them
content:
This chapter summarizes recently added features and, above all, WHERE they are enabled in the interface.

1. "Fit → Advanced fit options" menu

  Groups the optimizer's fine settings:

  • Line profile: Lorentzian or Voigt (the Voigt is normalized to its peak analytically, independent of sampling). σ (global Gaussian width) is only active with the Voigt profile; use the Fit σ checkbox next to the σ slider to fit σ as a free parameter.
  • Likelihood: Gauss (χ², data σ; default) or Poisson (IRLS, model σ). Poisson rescales the uncertainty from the model at each iteration.
  • Robust loss: controls how the optimizer treats large residuals (isolated bad channels, spurious spikes, folding defects or poorly normalized points).
      - Linear: classical least squares. Recommended for clean spectra and for final comparable results; all channels are weighted according to σ.
      - Soft L1: smooth transition between quadratic and linear behavior. Usually the most conservative robust option when only a few points look suspicious.
      - Huber: quadratic near zero and linear for large residuals. Useful when there are clear outliers.
    Important: it does not fix an incomplete physical model or bad calibration/folding. If enabling a robust loss changes the parameters strongly, inspect the responsible channels and report which loss was used.
  • Propagate calibration σ (vmax): adds the velocity-scale uncertainty in quadrature to the weights; realistically inflates the δ and BHF errors.
  • Global optimization (DE) before TRF: a differential-evolution pre-pass to escape local minima in multi-sextet spectra (slower; optional).
  • P(BHF): add active sharp components; free global distribution parameters are refined according to their fixed checkboxes.
  • Absorber: Thin (linear, default) or Thick (saturation). In thick mode a
    saturation parameter C is added:
      T(v) = b + sv − C·(1 − exp(−A_total(v)/C))
    where C→∞ recovers the thin model. Thick mode corrects biased areas and
    artificially broadened lines caused by high-absorption samples. Use when the
    absorption depth exceeds ~15–20% or when the thin-model residual is structured
    at the line minima.
    Reference: Margulies, S. & Ehrman, J.R. (1961). Nucl. Instrum. Methods 12, 131.
    Full details: docs/correccion_espesor.pdf

2. Context menus (right-click) on the sextet sliders

  The intensity mode and the quadrupole treatment are chosen with the RIGHT mouse button on the relevant sliders:

  • Right-click on I13/I23/I or the t slider → "Intensity mode": Free (independent i1,i2,i3) or Texture (a single parameter t = sin²θ giving 3 : 4t/(2−t) : 1; t=2/3 yields the 3:2:1 random-powder ratio).
  • Right-click on β or ΔEQ → "Quadrupole treatment": 1st order (rigid pattern, historic), Kündig fixed (diagonalizes the Hamiltonian with free β) or Kündig powder (polycrystalline average).

  Kündig quadrupole treatment:
    1st order adds ΔEQ as a rigid correction to the sextet line positions
    (±ΔEQ/2 on the outer lines, ∓ΔEQ/2 on the inner lines).
    Kündig fixed and powder fully diagonalise the combined magnetic+quadrupole
    Hamiltonian:
      H = ω_L·I_z + (ΔEQ/6)·(3I_{z'}² − I(I+1))
    where β is the angle between BHF and the principal axis of the EFG.
    In Kündig fixed, β is a free parameter (0°–90°). In Kündig powder the
    spectrum is averaged over all orientations β (Gauss-Legendre quadrature).
    The EFG asymmetry parameter η is assumed = 0 (axially symmetric EFG).
    The Kündig treatment is important when ΔEQ is large or comparable to the
    magnetic line separation.
    Reference: Kündig, W. (1967). Evaluation of the electric-field gradient
    and the asymmetry parameter η in Mössbauer spectra.
    Nucl. Instrum. Methods 48, 219–228.

  Parameters not used for the current type or mode are shown greyed out (disabled): e.g. a singlet disables ΔEQ, BHF and the unused widths/intensities; Texture mode disables i1,i2,i3 leaving only t; β is only active with "Kündig fixed".

3. P(BHF) / P(ΔEQ) distribution

  • Regularization: in the distribution panel choose Tikhonov (smooth, second difference) or Total Variation (TV, sharp edges).
  • α selection: the L-curve dialog proposes three values — L-curve corner, compromise and GCV (generalized cross-validation) — with a button to apply each.
  • The 1σ band around P is drawn automatically (clipped to P≥0).
  • Reduced χ² uses effective degrees of freedom, not the raw bin count.

4. Simulation behavior

  • When you load a file NO model is drawn: only the data are shown. The simulation appears as soon as you touch a model parameter (slider/entry), run a fit, or load a session.
  • "Initialize from minima" (Fit menu) detects the subspectra, activates the corresponding components, and scales the depths so the initial model matches the data level.


5. Quick calibration from the sample box

  In the file/sample box, RIGHT-CLICK opens a menu to use the loaded spectrum as calibration:

  • "Use as calibration (current vmax & iso)": sets the calibration taking directly the current velocity (vmax) and isomer shift (δ of the first active sextet), with no dialog.
  • "Use as calibration (details)...": opens the dialog with editable fields (name, vmax, iso).

6. Corrected isomer shift

  If a calibration with a reference iso is active, the program computes the CORRECTED δ of each component:

    δ_corr = δ_measured − iso_calibration

  which shifts the velocity zero to the calibration standard (e.g. α-Fe). It appears both in the results box (a "corrected δ" line per component) and in the Markdown/PDF report (a "Corrected δ" section). Shown only when the calibration carries a reference iso.

title: Command-line fitting
heading: CLI: template + spectrum → result JSON
content:
The mossbauer_fit_cli.py script lets you fit one spectrum outside the GUI from a template file (a saved session .json). Useful to process many heterogeneous files in shell loops, embed fits in a pipeline, or reproduce results without opening the interface.

Basic usage:

  python mossbauer_fit_cli.py \
      --template template.json \
      --spectrum measurement.adt \
      --out result.json

Arguments:

  --template   Template .json file. Either a full session saved from the
               GUI (File ▸ Save session...) or a JSON with just the
               model_state block.
  --spectrum   Spectrum .ws5 or .adt to fit.
  --out        Output .json path.
  --vmax       (optional) Overrides the template's vmax.
  --quiet      Suppress the stdout summary (exit code only).

Exit codes:

  0   fit OK.
  1   error (missing file, fit failure, etc.).

Examples:

  # One compound, hand-made template
  python mossbauer_fit_cli.py --template T.json --spectrum a.adt --out a.fit.json

  # Many different files, each with its own template, from shell
  for f in data/*.adt; do
      python mossbauer_fit_cli.py \
          --template templates/$(basename "$f" .adt).json \
          --spectrum "$f" \
          --out results/$(basename "$f" .adt).fit.json
  done

  # Headless server (no display)
  xvfb-run -a python mossbauer_fit_cli.py \
      --template T.json --spectrum a.adt --out a.fit.json

Output JSON format:

  Same structure as a saved GUI session (counts, model_state, last_fit, calibration, ...) plus a batch_fit_result section with:
    • spectrum, template: paths used.
    • values: final values of all active parameters.
    • errors: 1σ errors per free parameter.
    • stats: chi2, red_chi2, dof, AIC, BIC, residual diagnostics.
    • free_keys: list of fitted parameters.

  Can be reloaded as a session in the GUI or reused as a template for the next fit.

Ready-to-use templates in data_sample/:

  template_alphaFe.json     Metallic α-Fe (sextet, BHF≈33 T)
  template_hematita.json    Hematite α-Fe2O3 (sextet, BHF≈51.5 T, 2ε≈-0.20)
  template_magnetita.json   Magnetite Fe3O4 (two sextets, sites A and B)
  template_siderita.json    Siderite FeCO3 (paramagnetic Fe²⁺ doublet)

Building a template by hand:

  Easiest path: load a representative spectrum in the GUI, fit (or set parameters manually), and do File ▸ Save session... — the resulting .json is a valid template. The files in data_sample/ are minimal editable examples.

  Minimum expected structure (model_state):
    • vars            initial values of all model parameters.
    • fixed           which parameters stay fixed during the fit.
    • sextet_enabled  which components (1, 2, 3, ...) are active.
    • component_kind  type per component: Sextete / Doblete / Singlete.
    • intensity_mode  free or texture, per component.
    • quad_treatment  1st_order, kundig_fixed or kundig_powder, per component.
    • line_profile    Lorentziana or Voigt.
    • likelihood      gauss or poisson.
    • robust_loss     linear, soft_l1 or huber.
    • constraints     list of linear constraints (optional).

  The folding center is NOT taken from the template: each spectrum auto-detects its own. vmax is (calibration); can be overridden with --vmax.

title: What is new
heading: New functions since version 0.2
content:
Recent improvements include:

  • Poisson-weighted fitting for discrete models and distributions.
  • Reduced χ², AIC and BIC.
  • Numerical areas for Lorentzian and Voigt profiles.
  • Parameter correlation summaries.
  • Extended L-curve with reduced χ² and exportable table.
  • Residual diagnostics.
  • Deterministic multi-start fitting.
  • Progress windows for long calculations.
  • Markdown/PDF report export.
  • Bootstrap Monte Carlo errors for discrete fits.
  • Physical constraint presets.
  • Optional folding-point fitting.
  • Calibration uncertainty traceability when available.
v0.4.2 — Local-file calibration:

  New menu item File → "Use current file as calibration...".
  If you load a .ws5 or .adt that turns out to be a calibration spectrum
  (α-Fe or another velocity standard), you can now tell the program without
  having to download it from the web database. The dialog asks for:
    • Sample: standard name (pre-filled with the file name).
    • Vmax (mm/s): if provided, applied immediately to the active spectrum
      (same as a web-sourced calibration).
    • IS (mm/s): isomer shift of the standard (informational only).
  On confirmation the program records the calibration with source="local"
  (as opposed to "web_api" for API-sourced calibrations). The information
  is saved in the JSON session and appears in the info panel and report.
v0.4.3 — Active calibration display:

  A new label in the file panel (just below the spectrum name) always
  shows which calibration is active and its parameters:
    Line 1 — Calibration [local] or [web_api] and sample name.
    Line 2 — Vmax and IS (if available).
  The label hides when a new file is loaded and reappears when a session
  with saved calibration is restored.

  Also, the 'Use current file as calibration...' dialog now pre-fills
  the Vmax field with the current slider value and the IS field with the
  δ of the first active sextet, so you can confirm with one click if the
  fit is already done.
v0.4.4 — Persistent calibration across file loads:

  Bug fix: opening a new .ws5/.adt file no longer clears the active
  calibration. The calibration reflects the instrument state (velocity
  scale), not the data file, and now persists until the user explicitly
  sets a new one via the local calibration dialog or web download.
v0.4.5 — Extended BHF and AEQ ranges:

  • BHF up to 60 T: hyperfine field sliders in discrete sextets,
    fixed BHF in distribution mode and B min of the integration range.
  • Negative AEQ: AEQ (sextets) and fixed ΔEQ (distribution) sliders
    change from [0, 4] to [−4, 4] mm/s, allowing negative electric
    field gradients.
v0.4.6 — Sample data and improved file filter:

  • The data_sample/ folder included in the ZIP contains two ready-to-
    open example spectra: calibration.adt (α-Fe calibration) and
    Fe3O4.adt (magnetite), each with a pre-fitted session file.
    To use them: File → Open... the .adt, then
    File → Load session... the corresponding .json.
  • The open dialog now also accepts *.WS5 and *.ADT (uppercase),
    useful on Windows where file names may be uppercase.
v0.4.7 — Automatic pip on update and startup:

  • After installing an update, the program automatically runs
    pip install -r requirements.txt and shows the result in the
    final message.
  • At startup, if requirements.txt is newer than the last recorded
    check, pip runs in the background without interrupting the user.
    Keeps dependencies up to date without any manual steps.
v0.4.8 — Line profile from the σ slider:

  • Right-click the σ slider to switch between the two line profiles
    (Lorentzian and Voigt) without opening the menu.
v0.4.9 — Asymmetric profile-likelihood errors:

  • New Fit → Error analysis → Profile-likelihood errors... For every free parameter of
    the last discrete fit, profiles Δχ²(p) (fixing p, refitting the
    rest) and locates Δχ²=1 (1σ) and Δχ²=4 (2σ) crossings, producing
    asymmetric +Δ⁺/−Δ⁻ bars.
  • Useful when the likelihood is not parabolic, with strong correlations
    (σ–γ, BHF–δ in overlapping sextets), or when the fit hits a physical
    bound. Complementary to the bootstrap MC.
  • Asymmetric bars appear in the Status panel and the Markdown/PDF
    report; on completion a window shows the Δχ²(p) curves with the
    Δχ²=1 and 4 cut lines.
v0.4.10 — Batch fit (sequential warm-start):

  • New Fit → Batch fit (series)... Select N .ws5/.adt files and fit
    them one by one, using the active model as the template and the
    previous fit's result as the starting point of the next one
    (warm-start). Useful for temperature/field series.
  • Optional metadata extraction (T, B...) from the file name using a
    regex with a (?P<v>...) group.
  • Failure-tolerant: if a file fails to load/fit it is marked as
    "failed" and the series continues.
  • Outputs: TSV export with values and errors per spectrum, and a
    trend window plotting each free parameter vs metadata (with error
    bars).

v0.4.11 — NORMOS compatibility and velocity axis:

  • Signed Vmax is accepted and preserved in the GUI, CLI and web calibrations.
  • The depth slider is 0–0.07 for fine manual control, while the internal fit bound stays wider.
  • Defaults: component-1 depth = 0.02 and Γ = 0.15 mm/s.
  • int3 is hidden/fixed to 1; int1≈D13 and int2≈D23 follow the NORMOS convention.
  • Right-click σ to switch Lorentzian/Voigt.
  • When the first and last folded channels are trimmed, the original velocity axis is trimmed too instead of being rebuilt to ±Vmax. This avoids velocity-scale stretching and systematic BHF bias.
